selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You know, it's interesting that people ask me about fake content on social media because I actually don't use any of those platforms myself. I made that choice deliberately a while back, and honestly, it's been one of the better decisions I've made. But I understand why it matters, especially since my partner and friends are always showing me things they've seen online, and sometimes I can tell right away that something doesn't seem right. From what I observe from the outside looking in, the biggest problem is that people consume information so quickly that they don't pause to question what they're seeing. When my curious nature kicks in and someone shows me something surprising, my first instinct is always to ask where it came from or whether it makes logical sense. That's just how I approach things naturally. I think people need to slow down and engage that same questioning mindset. In my work as a sales associate here in Lecce, I've learned that not everything presented to you is exactly as it appears, and you need to verify information before making decisions. The same principle applies to content online. I'd suggest people look for multiple sources before believing something, especially if it triggers strong emotions. Fake content usually tries to make you angry or scared or overly excited because that's how it spreads. Another thing I've noticed is that not being on social media actually protects me from a lot of this nonsense. I get my news from more traditional sources, I talk to real people, and I have more time for things I genuinely enjoy like cycling around the beautiful Salento countryside or playing football with friends. My children are still very young, but when they grow up, I want to teach them to think critically about information rather than just accepting everything at face value. I believe the real solution isn't just about spotting fake content but questioning whether we need to be so immersed in these platforms in the first place. Sometimes stepping back entirely gives you the clearest perspective on what's real and what matters.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 24-year-old with primary school education but high openness (curious, receptive to new ideas) and low extraversion (prefers solitary activities, not socially driven). Critically, he has NO social media presence. This creates an interesting outsider perspective. His curiosity would make him naturally skeptical and questioning. His work as a sales associate gives him practical experience with persuasion and verification. Being from Lecce, Italy, he has local context. Having two children at 24 with primary education suggests practical life experience beyond formal schooling. His high agreeableness means he'd be understanding but his low neuroticism means calm, measured responses. His parents' higher education (bachelor's and PhD) might influence his awareness of critical thinking even if he didn't pursue higher education himself. The tone should be thoughtful, grounded, slightly removed (since he doesn't use social media), curious but practical, and humble about his perspective.
response_parsed.tone: thoughtful, grounded, observational, curious yet practical, humble, calm
